does: ConceptGuard introduces a neuro-symbolic safety framework that uses Sparse Autoencoders to identify and extract interpretable concepts from LLM internal activations that correspond to jailbreak attacks. The method discovers semantically meaningful sparse features within transformer hidden states that capture different jailbreak themes, then constructs safety guardrails that detect and mitigate harmful prompts by monitoring these interpretable concept activations. The framework operates without fine-tuning or modifying the model weights, instead building defensive mechanisms through post-hoc analysis of the model's internal representational geometry. The work provides evidence that jailbreak attacks across different themes share a common activation structure, enabling generalizable detection across attack variations.
connects: This work builds on two converging research directions: mechanistic interpretability research that seeks to understand LLM internals through sparse feature decomposition, and adversarial robustness work on alignment and jailbreak defenses. It extends prior alignment research beyond parameter-efficient fine-tuning and safety training by leveraging recent advances in Sparse Autoencoders for discovering monosemantic features in transformer activations. The paper responds to limitations of existing safety mechanisms like RLHF and safety fine-tuning that provide only partial robustness against adaptive attacks. It also connects to work on concept-based interpretability and explainable AI systems, bringing interpretability directly into the safety alignment problem rather than treating these as separate concerns.
enables: ConceptGuard's approach enables several future directions in interpretable AI safety. The framework makes possible the construction of fully explainable safety systems where interventions can be justified through the discovered jailbreak concepts, supporting transparency and auditability in deployed systems. The extraction of shared activation geometry across jailbreak attacks could lead to more principled understanding of what makes LLMs vulnerable, potentially informing better alignment strategies from first principles. The method opens pathways for generalizable defenses that work across model families and attack variations without retraining, and suggests that sparse mechanistic interpretability could become a core technique for building robust, trustworthy AI systems. Future work could extend this to other harmful behaviors beyond jailbreaks.
